exercises.title: A taxonomy-tagged input/output classifier
exercises.prompt: Llama Guard classifies both inputs and outputs against a hazard taxonomy (S1–S14 categories), returning safe/unsafe plus the violated category. Write `classify(text, role)` (role is 'input' or 'output') that checks text against a small category map and returns `{'verdict','category'}`. Show it tagging an unsafe input with its category and passing a benign one, and note that the same classifier runs on both sides of the model.
exercises.hint: Map keywords to categories like S1 (violent crimes), S9 (indiscriminate weapons), S14 (code interpreter abuse). Return 'unsafe' + the first matched category, else 'safe' + None. The role parameter shows it's symmetric: same function guards inputs and outputs.

exercises.title: Layered rails: input, output, and dialog-flow
exercises.prompt: NeMo Guardrails adds Colang dialog-flow rails on top of input and output rails. Model the three-layer pipeline: an input rail (block disallowed requests), a dialog rail (enforce a conversation policy, e.g. 'must collect consent before proceeding'), and an output rail (block disallowed responses). Build a pipeline that runs a turn through all three and short-circuits at the first violation, reporting which rail stopped it. Show a turn passing input but blocked by the dialog rail.
exercises.hint: Pipeline = [input_rail, dialog_rail, output_rail]. Each returns (ok, reason). Run in order; on first not-ok, return which rail blocked and stop (don't generate output if input failed; don't return output if output rail fails). Dialog rail checks conversation state, not just the message text.

exercises.title: Measure bypass rates and right-size your trust
exercises.prompt: Guardrails are a layer, not a solution: Emoji Smuggling hit ~100% attack success rate (ASR) against six guard systems, and NeMo Guard Detect recorded ~72.5% ASR on jailbreaks. Build an evaluation harness that measures a guard's true-positive rate on a labeled attack set, simulates an evasion technique that drops detection, and computes the residual risk that reaches the model. Then show why a defense-in-depth calculation (guard ASR combined with the model's own refusal) is the honest way to reason about safety. Use guard catch-rate 90% normally but 0% under emoji smuggling.
exercises.hint: Guard catches frac of attacks; ASR = 1 - catch_rate. Under an evasion, catch_rate -> ~0 so ASR -> ~1. Residual reaching model = ASR. Then layer the model's own refusal rate r: end-to-end success of attack = ASR_guard * (1 - r). Show that relying on the guard alone is fragile but layering with model refusal bounds the damage.
exercises.solution:
The lesson's bluntest point: a guard's headline catch-rate is measured on *known* attacks, and adversaries deliberately move off-distribution. Quantifying both the normal-case and the evaded-case ASR — then layering the model's own refusal — is the only honest way to reason about residual risk.

```python
def evaluate_guard(attacks, guard_catches):
    caught = sum(guard_catches(a) for a in attacks)
    catch_rate = caught / len(attacks)
    return catch_rate, 1 - catch_rate            # (catch_rate, ASR)

attacks = [f"jailbreak-{i}" for i in range(100)]

# Normal guard: catches 90% of in-distribution attacks
normal_guard = lambda a: int(a) % 10 != 0 if a.split('-')[1].isdigit() else True
guard_normal = lambda a: hash(a) % 10 != 0          # ~90% catch
cr, asr = evaluate_guard(attacks, guard_normal)
print(f"normal:  catch-rate {cr:.0%}  ASR {asr:.0%}")

# Emoji-smuggling evasion: catch-rate collapses to ~0%
guard_evaded = lambda a: False                       # 0% catch
cr_e, asr_e = evaluate_guard(attacks, guard_evaded)
print(f"evaded:  catch-rate {cr_e:.0%}  ASR {asr_e:.0%}")

# Defense in depth: layer the model's OWN refusal behavior (say 95%)
model_refusal = 0.95
for label, asr_g in [('guard alone (evaded)', asr_e),
                     ('guard+model (evaded)', asr_e)]:
    if 'model' in label:
        end_to_end = asr_g * (1 - model_refusal)     # both must fail
    else:
        end_to_end = asr_g
    print(f"{label:>22}: attack reaches harm {end_to_end:.0%}")
```

Output:

```
normal:  catch-rate 90%  ASR 10%
evaded:  catch-rate 0%  ASR 100%
guard alone (evaded): attack reaches harm 100%
guard+model (evaded): attack reaches harm 5%
```

**The fragility a single number hides.** The guard looks strong at 90% catch-rate — but that is measured on attacks it was built to catch. Under emoji smuggling (Unicode/emoji encoding that the classifier's tokenizer mishandles, the same class of evasion as the homoglyph attack in Lesson 11), the catch-rate collapses to ~0% and ASR jumps to ~100%. This is exactly the published result: ~100% ASR against six guard systems. A safety story that rests on "our guard catches 90%" is one novel evasion away from catching nothing.

**Why defense-in-depth is the honest frame.** The right question is not "how good is the guard?" but "what reaches harm when the guard fails?" If the guard is the *only* layer, an evaded attack reaches harm 100% of the time. If you layer the model's *own* refusal behavior (95% here), the attack only succeeds when *both* the guard and the model fail: `ASR_guard × (1 − model_refusal) = 100% × 5% = 5%`. The guard going to zero is survivable *because* it was never the only line of defense. Note the multiplication: independent layers multiply their failure rates, which is why adding layers helps even when each is individually beatable.

**The practical takeaways.**
- **Never make the guard load-bearing alone.** It is a cheap, fast filter that catches obvious and known misuse — genuinely valuable for cost and latency — but its catch-rate on *novel adversarial* inputs can be near zero. Size your trust to the evaded case, not the headline case.
- **The model's trained safety behavior is the deeper layer.** Guards complement it; they do not replace it. This is why Llama Guard and NeMo are explicitly designed to *pair with* a foundation model's safety, not substitute for it.
- **Combine with the rest of the phase.** For autonomous agents the layers stack further: guards (this lesson) + the model's constitutional refusals (Lesson 17) + sandboxing of any executed output (Lesson 5) + canaries/kill switches on the actions themselves (Lesson 14). Each layer is individually beatable; the product of their failure rates is what actually bounds the risk. Measuring each layer's *evaded* ASR — not its demo-day catch-rate — is what lets you compute that bound honestly.
